romania.title: Unul dintre cele mai populare seriale ale deceniului trecut continuă cu un nou „spin-off” lansat în streaming. Creatorul „Doi bărbați și jumătate”, interviu special pentru publicul HotNews
romania.summary: „The Big Bang Theory” a fost unul dintre cele mai populare și îndrăgite seriale ale deceniului trecut, umorul, personajele excentrice și numeroasele referințe la cultura pop transformându-l într-un fenomen al culturii pop de sine stătător. O nouă serie „spin-off”  numită „Stuart Fails to Save the Universe” își propune acum să recreeze acea magie. Porecla sa …

Vor fi concerte, spectacole,… Articolul Orașul Timișoara se pregătește de sărbătoare apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

Cea de-a șasea ediție a… Articolul Cluj-Napoca devine, din nou, capitala sportului accesibil și pentru persoanele cu dizabilități și din grupuri vulnerabile apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

Articolul Peace, Bro! apare prima dată în World Vision România .

Spre sfârşitul uneia dintre cele mai dificile etape, în comitatul Choucroute din Alpii francezi, pe o pantă de 7% şi într-o curbă cu vizibilitate redusă, 174 de ciclişti s-au […] The post Scandal la Turul Franţei: 174 de ciclişti, dărâmaţi de un cretin pe o trotinetă electrică appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.summary: București, 17 iulie 2026 – Teach for Romania a dat startul Academiei de Leadership și Formare în Educație, programul intensiv prin care pregătește cea de-a 13-a generație de viitori lideri în educație – directori, profesori, învățători și educatori, care să predea, începând cu anul școlar 2026-2027, și să transforme educația de la firul ierbii, în […]

medical.title: Potential new therapeutic targets for preeclampsia discovered
medical.summary: Unusual behavior in the cells of mothers and babies with preeclampsia may provide new therapeutic targets for the condition, a new UCL-led study finds.

medical.title: Inherited gene variants may shape CAR-T therapy benefits and toxic side effects
medical.summary: Chimeric antigen receptor (CAR)-T cell therapy, which reprograms an individual's immune cells to seek out and destroy certain cancer cells, has revolutionized treatment for blood cancers such as lymphoma. But in some patients, the treatment can cause serious side effects. New research led by investigators at the Mass General Brigham Cancer Institute, the Broad Institute of MIT and Harvard, and Dana-Farber Cancer Institute has shown that patients' inherited genetic makeup can influence whether they benefit from CAR-T cell therapy or experience toxicity from the treatment. The results are published in Science Immunology.

medical.title: Increased social media use linked to anxiety, suicidal ideation among young adults
medical.summary: Teenagers who spent six or more hours a day on social media were twice as likely to experience symptoms of anxiety and three times as likely to report suicidal ideation than those who spent less than one hour on social media each day, according to researchers at UTHealth Houston. The findings were published today in Pediatrics Open Science and led by Jeff Temple, Ph.D., professor and associate dean for clinical research at UTHealth Houston School of Behavioral Health Sciences.

medical.title: Scientists develop more photostable near-infrared fluorescent dye for longer surgical imaging
medical.summary: Researchers in Korea have developed a new near-infrared (NIR) fluorescent dye with significantly improved photostability, overcoming the rapid signal fading that has long limited fluorescence imaging during surgery for cancer, lymph node mapping and other medical procedures. The work is published in the journal Small.

medical.title: Tiny BAP1 mutations can disrupt internal signals that suppress tumor growth
medical.summary: Scientists at the Institute of Biochemical Sciences at National Taiwan University have uncovered how tiny genetic changes can disable one of the body's most important tumor-suppressing proteins. Their study, published in Nature Communications, reveals how cancer-associated mutations interfere with the function of BRCA1-associated protein 1 (BAP1), a protein that helps maintain normal cell growth and is frequently mutated in cancers such as mesothelioma, uveal melanoma and kidney cancer.

medical.title: Injectable biomaterial harnesses the immune system to promote brain repair after stroke
medical.summary: Biomedical engineers at Duke University have developed an injectable biomaterial that helps transform the cavity left behind after a stroke caused by a blood clot into an environment more supportive of repair. By recruiting the body's own immune cells, the treatment promoted the growth of new blood vessels, supported neural remodeling and improved motor performance in mice. The work appears in Cell Biomaterials.

medical.title: There are already more US measles cases this year than in all of 2025
medical.summary: The number of measles cases in the U.S. has surpassed the record-breaking tally for all of 2025, with five more months of 2026 still to come.

medical.title: Death of girl in Chinese gene-editing trial kept secret: Report
medical.summary: A 6-year-old Chinese girl died last year after receiving an experimental gene therapy to correct a non-life-threatening condition, according to an investigation by Science and Retraction Watch.

medical.title: New mechanoresistant cancer cells show how they survive high stress environments
medical.summary: When a cancer cell leaves the original tumor to spread to a new, faraway location, it first has to survive what most cells cannot: periodic high-force moments in the vessels and heart of up to 5,000 dynes per centimeter squared as it circulates throughout the body.

medical.title: New treatment approach improves survival, reduces recurrence risk in muscle-invasive bladder cancer
medical.summary: Researchers have found that a combination of enfortumab vedotin and pembrolizumab helps patients with muscle-invasive bladder cancer live longer while reducing the risk that their cancer will return after surgery, according to an international phase 3 clinical trial published in The New England Journal of Medicine.

medical.title: Red meat-related gut byproduct tied to higher atrial fibrillation risk
medical.summary: A Cleveland Clinic-led study shows a connection between elevated blood levels of TMAO (trimethylamine N-oxide)—a byproduct of gut bacteria digestion of nutrients found in red meat and other animal products—and a higher risk of atrial fibrillation.

medical.title: Biomarker discovery could advance treatments for obesity-related health risks
medical.summary: Researchers have identified a biological marker that rises and falls as body fat changes, offering new insight into how weight gain and weight loss may influence metabolic health.

medical.title: The mysterious hum heard around the world may finally have an explanation
medical.summary: The mysterious sound known as The Hum has been reported worldwide for decades, yet its source often remains impossible to find. Researchers tested whether people who hear it have exceptional low-frequency hearing or measurable sounds produced inside their ears, but neither explanation fit most participants. The strongest possibility is that many cases involve low-frequency tinnitus, even though the sound seems completely external.

medical.title: Popular weight-loss drugs Ozempic and Wegovy may slow biological aging
medical.summary: Researchers found that semaglutide, the active ingredient in Ozempic and Wegovy, slowed biological aging markers in adults with HIV, marking the first clinical evidence that the drug may influence human aging. Although the findings are encouraging, scientists say larger studies are needed before concluding that the medication can help people age more slowly.

medical.title: What positive change have you seen unfold over many years?
medical.summary: Share the stories of change you’ve witnessed that took patience, persistence and time The post What positive change have you seen unfold over many years? appeared first on Positive News .

science.title: Hydrogel electrode improves stability of insect-based odor sensors for drones
science.summary: Drones are increasingly being used for disaster response, infrastructure inspection and environmental monitoring. However, most drone systems rely primarily on cameras and other visual sensors, which can become ineffective in smoke, dust, darkness or collapsed structures. Odor sensing could provide a valuable complementary capability by enabling drones to detect gas leaks and chemical emissions that cannot be identified through visual sensing alone. Yet a major challenge has limited the practical use of such biohybrid sensors: Excised insect antennae gradually lose signal quality as they dry out, and the electrical interface becomes unstable.

science.title: Algorithms create foundry-ready photonic circuits
science.summary: Photonic microchips can process data at extremely high speeds and are embedded in a wide variety of today's technologies. Researchers at the Max Planck Institute for the Science of Light (MPL) and Harvard University have now succeeded in developing three functional components for such chips that are up to 500 times smaller than conventional designs. The researchers used inverse design, a computer algorithm, to achieve this. The results are published in Nature Communications.

science.title: How fishing may be changing one fish species at the molecular level
science.summary: The pearly razorfish (Xyrichtys novacula) is a small marine animal that lives in sandy coastal waters and feeds on creatures like shrimp. Known locally in Spain's Balearic Islands as raors, it is a popular dish in many homes and restaurants across the region.

science.title: Malaria parasite selectively consumes linoleic acid fats, revealing drug target potential
science.summary: Researchers at NYU Abu Dhabi have discovered that the malaria parasite relies on a specific type of fat to survive and multiply within the human host, uncovering a previously unknown vulnerability that could help guide future treatments.

science.title: Review finds lack of evidence as to whether planting around our homes reduces the pollution we breathe
science.summary: A new review led by the University of East London (UEL) has uncovered a major blind spot in air pollution research. Despite domestic gardens covering vast areas of Britain's towns and cities, researchers found no direct evidence showing whether they reduce pollution levels or improve the air people breathe at home. There is a huge gap in understanding the role of domestic gardens in air quality.

science.title: Rocks from unexplored far side of the moon help rewrite lunar history
science.summary: Ancient samples from the moon's far side are helping scientists uncover how asteroid impacts shaped the early solar system. The findings challenge decades of thinking about when those impacts occurred and offer new insights into the conditions that influenced Earth's evolution.

science.title: Trust among farmers is key to scaling climate-smart innovations
science.summary: As the climate becomes increasingly variable and extreme events become more frequent, accelerating the use of innovations that can strengthen agricultural resilience has become a priority for agrifood systems. Science has made progress in developing solutions with the potential to help farmers address these challenges. However, ensuring that knowledge about these innovations reaches more farmers and is understood, adapted and scaled remains one of the major challenges in research for development.

science.title: Fat-storing cell structures may block viral spread by recruiting antiviral proteins
science.summary: La Trobe University scientists have uncovered the critical role fatty cell structures play in our body's early detection and defense against viruses. Published in Nature Communications, the discovery could open the door to a new type of antiviral strategy that harnesses the body's innate defense mechanisms rather than targeting individual viruses.

science.title: Why and how birdsong differs across the world—and the eight motifs that tie them together
science.summary: Birdsong carries meaning across open space, helping birds attract mates, defend territory and communicate with rivals. While there has been much research into the use of birdsong across species, researchers have lacked a single global framework that explains birdsong complexity and how it differs from place to place. But now, in a study recently published in Science, a group of researchers in France have analyzed more than 116,000 bird songs from 3,160 passerine species around the world, allowing them to identify eight common motifs and explain differences in birdsong.

science.title: New Crew Members Welcomed to International Space Station
science.summary: From left, Expedition 74 flight engineers Anna Kikina and Pyotr Dubrov of Roscosmos, and Anil Menon of NASA pose for a July 18, 2026, photo while holding a cake celebrating their recent arrival aboard the International Space Station. The trio arrived at the space station on July 14, 2026, after launching from the Baikonur Cosmodrome […]

science.title: Quantum Zeno effect could freeze computations as qubit systems scale up
science.summary: The promise of quantum computing is to solve complex problems faster and more energy-efficiently than today's supercomputers—from optimizing logistics to simulating molecules. This goal is coming within reach as the number of qubits—the computational units of quantum computing—increases.

science.title: Magnetic clues inside atomic nuclei help explain how elements form in stars
science.summary: A scientific team led by Facility for Rare Isotope Beams, or FRIB, has identified the origin of a mysterious excess of low-energy gamma rays emitted by the nucleus zinc-70. They found that the excess is caused by magnetic transitions within the nucleus. The study, "Magnetic Character of the Low-Energy Enhancement in 70Zn," published in Nature, sheds light on a long-standing puzzle in nuclear physics and has far-reaching implications for astrophysics.

science.title: Community context shapes corporate response to violence in street protests
science.summary: When violence takes place during street protests, how do corporate leaders respond? According to new research by Forrest Briscoe, professor of organizational behavior at the ILR School, the answer depends on other community characteristics that people can use to help interpret the reasons for that violence. In particular, Briscoe focused on whether the community has a long history of protests turning violent regardless of the issue being protested—and, separately, whether the community has a history of events that validate the protestors' main grievance.

science.title: Why Europa's hidden ocean may be more difficult to reach than scientists thought
science.summary: Europa, one of Jupiter's icy moons, has long fascinated scientists because of what may lie beneath its frozen shell: a global ocean of liquid water. That hidden ocean has made Europa one of the most compelling places in the solar system to study the conditions that might support life. But new research led by Rutgers scientist Lujendra Ojha suggests one of the most promising shortcuts to Europa's ocean may be far more complicated than previously thought.

science.title: Io's volcanic output offers a 40‑day forecast of plasma density in Jupiter's magnetosphere
science.summary: A careful statistical comparison of observations recorded by NASA's Juno mission and the Planetary Science Institute's Io Input Output observatory (IoIO) shows that IoIO observations reliably predict the density of Jupiter's plasma disk, an important part of Jupiter's magnetosphere, according to a new Geophysical Research Letters paper co-authored by Planetary Science Institute Senior Scientist Jeff Morgenthaler. Jian-Zhao Wang of the University of Colorado Boulder is the paper's lead author.

science.title: Researchers develop AI-driven method to improve forecasts weeks in advance
science.summary: Haonan Chen, a CSU associate professor of electrical and computer engineering, and his team have published research that could improve the accuracy of precipitation forecasts up to two weeks in advance.

science.title: NASA to Support Blue Origin New Glenn Rocket Testing, Advance Artemis
science.summary: NASA and Blue Origin announced Friday they recently agreed to conduct second stage hot fire testing for the company’s New Glenn rocket on the B-2 test stand at the agency’s Stennis Space Center near Bay St. Louis, Mississippi. The annex to a reimbursable Space Act Agreement signed earlier this month highlights NASA’s commitment to working […]

science.title: Why evolution never produced mammal teeth that slice and crush equally well
science.summary: Evolution is all about trade-offs. Traits that may be beneficial in one situation may backfire in another. Over millions of years, species can become specialized for one way of living rather than being equally good at everything. This explains why mammal teeth look the way they do today.

science.title: UK in pole position to lead key instrument in NASA's hunt for Earth-like worlds
science.summary: UK astronomers are a step closer to playing a leading role in NASA's search for alien life in the coming decades as they continue developing one of the core instruments of the Habitable Worlds Observatory (HWO). The exciting mission—the U.S. space agency's next flagship space telescope after the Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope—is expected to launch in the 2040s.

science.title: Metal-ion coating improves extracellular vesicles for cancer detection and targeted delivery
science.summary: Researchers, including those from the University of Tokyo, found a way to optimize how cells bind to small packages they release called extracellular vesicles. By coating the vesicles with metal ions, they made cells and their corresponding vesicles stick together more strongly than they would naturally. This reduced the time needed for cells to capture their own vesicles, even in mixtures containing billions of other vesicles.

science.title: A tiny universe in a bottle reveals clues to the origins of life
science.summary: Researchers have created cosmic dust from scratch by recreating space-like conditions inside glass tubes. The dust contains complex carbon-rich molecules built from elements essential to life and produces infrared signals similar to real material found in space. By studying these laboratory samples, scientists can explore how organic chemistry unfolds around stars and how comets, asteroids and meteorites may have carried those ingredients to Earth.

environment.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
environment.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

environment.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
environment.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

environment.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
environment.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

environment.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
environment.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

environment.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
environment.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

environment.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
environment.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

environment.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
environment.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

environment.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
environment.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

mix_items.title: Cluj-Napoca devine, din nou, capitala sportului accesibil și pentru persoanele cu dizabilități și din grupuri vulnerabile
mix_items.summary: Timp de șase săptămâni, între 3 august și 13 septembrie, sute de beneficiari vor participa gratuit la antrenamente de caiac, baschet, tenis și echitație adaptată. Cea de-a șasea ediție a… Articolul Cluj-Napoca devine, din nou, capitala sportului accesibil și pentru persoanele cu dizabilități și din grupuri vulnerabile apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: Unul dintre cele mai populare seriale ale deceniului trecut continuă cu un nou „spin-off” lansat în streaming. Creatorul „Doi bărbați și jumătate”, interviu special pentru publicul HotNews
mix_items.summary: „The Big Bang Theory” a fost unul dintre cele mai populare și îndrăgite seriale ale deceniului trecut, umorul, personajele excentrice și numeroasele referințe la cultura pop transformându-l într-un fenomen al culturii pop de sine stătător. O nouă serie „spin-off”  numită „Stuart Fails to Save the Universe” își propune acum să recreeze acea magie. Porecla sa …

mix_items.title: Peace, Bro!
mix_items.summary: Proiect implementat de: Asociația Centrul Cultural al Romilor „O Del Amenca” - Copiii, părinții și profesorii au învățat împreună să recunoască, să prevină și să combată bullyingul, contribuind la construirea unor școli mai sigure și mai incluzive. Articolul Peace, Bro! apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Harmony – Viitorul începe cu respect
mix_items.summary: Asociația Împreună pentru Viitor (Constanța) - Educație, colaborare și empatie pentru un mediu școlar mai sigur. Articolul Harmony – Viitorul începe cu respect apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: 😂 S-a molipsit? De când s-a pupat cu un urmărit internaţional, Ciolacu dă şi el semne de urmărire internaţională
mix_items.summary: Doamne fereşte să nu ne trezim pe cap cu încă o pandemie! Prietenii sunt îngrijoraţi pentru fostul şef PSD, Marcel Ciolacu, după ce acesta s-a întâlnit în Italia cu colegul său Ionel Arsene, deşi acesta din urmă era urmărit internaţional. Câteva zile mai târziu, Ciolacu a început să dea şi el semne de urmărire internaţională, […] The post S-a molipsit? De când s-a pupat cu un urmărit internaţional, Ciolacu dă şi el semne de urmărire internaţională appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: Mediu școlar mai sigur pentru copii
mix_items.summary: Asociația pentru Dezvoltare Comunitară Vaslui - Școli mai sigure pentru fiecare copil, prin educație și responsabilitate comună. Articolul Mediu școlar mai sigur pentru copii apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: „WEBTECTIVII”
mix_items.summary: Asociația The Da Vinci System (Cluj) - Educație pentru siguranța copiilor în mediul online. Articolul „WEBTECTIVII” apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: 😂 Scandal la Turul Franţei: 174 de ciclişti, dărâmaţi de un cretin pe o trotinetă electrică
mix_items.summary: Celebrul Tur al Franţei a fost întrerupt şi ar putea fi anulat complet pentru că toată lumea spune că îşi bagă picioarele după incidentul de azi! Spre sfârşitul uneia dintre cele mai dificile etape, în comitatul Choucroute din Alpii francezi, pe o pantă de 7% şi într-o curbă cu vizibilitate redusă, 174 de ciclişti s-au […] The post Scandal la Turul Franţei: 174 de ciclişti, dărâmaţi de un cretin pe o trotinetă electrică appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: Schimbarea educației din România de jos în sus, prin pregătirea generației a 13-a de profesori-soluție pentru școli din mediul rural.
mix_items.summary: București, 17 iulie 2026 – Teach for Romania a dat startul Academiei de Leadership și Formare în Educație, programul intensiv prin care pregătește cea de-a 13-a generație de viitori lideri în educație – directori, profesori, învățători și educatori, care să predea, începând cu anul școlar 2026-2027, și să transforme educația de la firul ierbii, în […]

mix_items.title: Aproape 100.000 de copii învață în clase simultane. World Vision România propune o soluție națională pentru profesorii care predau mai multor clase în aceeași oră.
mix_items.summary: București, 16 iulie. Aproape 100.000 de elevi din România învață în clase organizate în regim simultan, majoritatea în mediul rural,... Articolul Aproape 100.000 de copii învață în clase simultane. World Vision România propune o soluție națională pentru profesorii care predau mai multor clase în aceeași oră. apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Inocenți, locul în care curajul crește pas cu pas
mix_items.summary: Descoperă povestea Alinei și cum sprijinul oferit de Fundația Inocenți a ajutat-o să își dezvolte încrederea în sine, abilitățile școlare și curajul de a reuși. The post Inocenți, locul în care curajul crește pas cu pas appeared first on Fundația Inocenți .

mix_items.title: Potential new therapeutic targets for preeclampsia discovered
mix_items.summary: Unusual behavior in the cells of mothers and babies with preeclampsia may provide new therapeutic targets for the condition, a new UCL-led study finds.

mix_items.title: Părinte Social
mix_items.summary: Părinte Social (Mamă SOS) – București și Cisnădie, Sibiu Transformă un loc de muncă într-o misiune de viață La SOS Satele Copiilor România, credem că fiecare copil are dreptul să... The post Părinte Social appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: Psiholog
mix_items.summary: Locație: Bacău | Nivel carieră: Experiență de minimum 1 an Candidatul Ideal: Ești tu vocea de care au nevoie? Un copil are nevoie de iubire, siguranță și încredere pentru a... The post Psiholog appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: Hydrogel electrode improves stability of insect-based odor sensors for drones
mix_items.summary: Drones are increasingly being used for disaster response, infrastructure inspection and environmental monitoring. However, most drone systems rely primarily on cameras and other visual sensors, which can become ineffective in smoke, dust, darkness or collapsed structures. Odor sensing could provide a valuable complementary capability by enabling drones to detect gas leaks and chemical emissions that cannot be identified through visual sensing alone. Yet a major challenge has limited the practical use of such biohybrid sensors: Excised insect antennae gradually lose signal quality as they dry out, and the electrical interface becomes unstable.

mix_items.title: Inherited gene variants may shape CAR-T therapy benefits and toxic side effects
mix_items.summary: Chimeric antigen receptor (CAR)-T cell therapy, which reprograms an individual's immune cells to seek out and destroy certain cancer cells, has revolutionized treatment for blood cancers such as lymphoma. But in some patients, the treatment can cause serious side effects. New research led by investigators at the Mass General Brigham Cancer Institute, the Broad Institute of MIT and Harvard, and Dana-Farber Cancer Institute has shown that patients' inherited genetic makeup can influence whether they benefit from CAR-T cell therapy or experience toxicity from the treatment. The results are published in Science Immunology.

mix_items.title: Increased social media use linked to anxiety, suicidal ideation among young adults
mix_items.summary: Teenagers who spent six or more hours a day on social media were twice as likely to experience symptoms of anxiety and three times as likely to report suicidal ideation than those who spent less than one hour on social media each day, according to researchers at UTHealth Houston. The findings were published today in Pediatrics Open Science and led by Jeff Temple, Ph.D., professor and associate dean for clinical research at UTHealth Houston School of Behavioral Health Sciences.

mix_items.title: Algorithms create foundry-ready photonic circuits
mix_items.summary: Photonic microchips can process data at extremely high speeds and are embedded in a wide variety of today's technologies. Researchers at the Max Planck Institute for the Science of Light (MPL) and Harvard University have now succeeded in developing three functional components for such chips that are up to 500 times smaller than conventional designs. The researchers used inverse design, a computer algorithm, to achieve this. The results are published in Nature Communications.

mix_items.title: How fishing may be changing one fish species at the molecular level
mix_items.summary: The pearly razorfish (Xyrichtys novacula) is a small marine animal that lives in sandy coastal waters and feeds on creatures like shrimp. Known locally in Spain's Balearic Islands as raors, it is a popular dish in many homes and restaurants across the region.

mix_items.title: Malaria parasite selectively consumes linoleic acid fats, revealing drug target potential
mix_items.summary: Researchers at NYU Abu Dhabi have discovered that the malaria parasite relies on a specific type of fat to survive and multiply within the human host, uncovering a previously unknown vulnerability that could help guide future treatments.

mix_items.title: Scientists develop more photostable near-infrared fluorescent dye for longer surgical imaging
mix_items.summary: Researchers in Korea have developed a new near-infrared (NIR) fluorescent dye with significantly improved photostability, overcoming the rapid signal fading that has long limited fluorescence imaging during surgery for cancer, lymph node mapping and other medical procedures. The work is published in the journal Small.

mix_items.title: Review finds lack of evidence as to whether planting around our homes reduces the pollution we breathe
mix_items.summary: A new review led by the University of East London (UEL) has uncovered a major blind spot in air pollution research. Despite domestic gardens covering vast areas of Britain's towns and cities, researchers found no direct evidence showing whether they reduce pollution levels or improve the air people breathe at home. There is a huge gap in understanding the role of domestic gardens in air quality.

mix_items.title: Rocks from unexplored far side of the moon help rewrite lunar history
mix_items.summary: Ancient samples from the moon's far side are helping scientists uncover how asteroid impacts shaped the early solar system. The findings challenge decades of thinking about when those impacts occurred and offer new insights into the conditions that influenced Earth's evolution.

mix_items.title: Tiny BAP1 mutations can disrupt internal signals that suppress tumor growth
mix_items.summary: Scientists at the Institute of Biochemical Sciences at National Taiwan University have uncovered how tiny genetic changes can disable one of the body's most important tumor-suppressing proteins. Their study, published in Nature Communications, reveals how cancer-associated mutations interfere with the function of BRCA1-associated protein 1 (BAP1), a protein that helps maintain normal cell growth and is frequently mutated in cancers such as mesothelioma, uveal melanoma and kidney cancer.

mix_items.title: Injectable biomaterial harnesses the immune system to promote brain repair after stroke
mix_items.summary: Biomedical engineers at Duke University have developed an injectable biomaterial that helps transform the cavity left behind after a stroke caused by a blood clot into an environment more supportive of repair. By recruiting the body's own immune cells, the treatment promoted the growth of new blood vessels, supported neural remodeling and improved motor performance in mice. The work appears in Cell Biomaterials.

mix_items.title: There are already more US measles cases this year than in all of 2025
mix_items.summary: The number of measles cases in the U.S. has surpassed the record-breaking tally for all of 2025, with five more months of 2026 still to come.

mix_items.title: Trust among farmers is key to scaling climate-smart innovations
mix_items.summary: As the climate becomes increasingly variable and extreme events become more frequent, accelerating the use of innovations that can strengthen agricultural resilience has become a priority for agrifood systems. Science has made progress in developing solutions with the potential to help farmers address these challenges. However, ensuring that knowledge about these innovations reaches more farmers and is understood, adapted and scaled remains one of the major challenges in research for development.

mix_items.title: Fat-storing cell structures may block viral spread by recruiting antiviral proteins
mix_items.summary: La Trobe University scientists have uncovered the critical role fatty cell structures play in our body's early detection and defense against viruses. Published in Nature Communications, the discovery could open the door to a new type of antiviral strategy that harnesses the body's innate defense mechanisms rather than targeting individual viruses.

mix_items.title: Why and how birdsong differs across the world—and the eight motifs that tie them together
mix_items.summary: Birdsong carries meaning across open space, helping birds attract mates, defend territory and communicate with rivals. While there has been much research into the use of birdsong across species, researchers have lacked a single global framework that explains birdsong complexity and how it differs from place to place. But now, in a study recently published in Science, a group of researchers in France have analyzed more than 116,000 bird songs from 3,160 passerine species around the world, allowing them to identify eight common motifs and explain differences in birdsong.

mix_items.title: New Crew Members Welcomed to International Space Station
mix_items.summary: From left, Expedition 74 flight engineers Anna Kikina and Pyotr Dubrov of Roscosmos, and Anil Menon of NASA pose for a July 18, 2026, photo while holding a cake celebrating their recent arrival aboard the International Space Station. The trio arrived at the space station on July 14, 2026, after launching from the Baikonur Cosmodrome […]

mix_items.title: Death of girl in Chinese gene-editing trial kept secret: Report
mix_items.summary: A 6-year-old Chinese girl died last year after receiving an experimental gene therapy to correct a non-life-threatening condition, according to an investigation by Science and Retraction Watch.

mix_items.title: Quantum Zeno effect could freeze computations as qubit systems scale up
mix_items.summary: The promise of quantum computing is to solve complex problems faster and more energy-efficiently than today's supercomputers—from optimizing logistics to simulating molecules. This goal is coming within reach as the number of qubits—the computational units of quantum computing—increases.

mix_items.title: New mechanoresistant cancer cells show how they survive high stress environments
mix_items.summary: When a cancer cell leaves the original tumor to spread to a new, faraway location, it first has to survive what most cells cannot: periodic high-force moments in the vessels and heart of up to 5,000 dynes per centimeter squared as it circulates throughout the body.

mix_items.title: Magnetic clues inside atomic nuclei help explain how elements form in stars
mix_items.summary: A scientific team led by Facility for Rare Isotope Beams, or FRIB, has identified the origin of a mysterious excess of low-energy gamma rays emitted by the nucleus zinc-70. They found that the excess is caused by magnetic transitions within the nucleus. The study, "Magnetic Character of the Low-Energy Enhancement in 70Zn," published in Nature, sheds light on a long-standing puzzle in nuclear physics and has far-reaching implications for astrophysics.

mix_items.title: Community context shapes corporate response to violence in street protests
mix_items.summary: When violence takes place during street protests, how do corporate leaders respond? According to new research by Forrest Briscoe, professor of organizational behavior at the ILR School, the answer depends on other community characteristics that people can use to help interpret the reasons for that violence. In particular, Briscoe focused on whether the community has a long history of protests turning violent regardless of the issue being protested—and, separately, whether the community has a history of events that validate the protestors' main grievance.

mix_items.title: New treatment approach improves survival, reduces recurrence risk in muscle-invasive bladder cancer
mix_items.summary: Researchers have found that a combination of enfortumab vedotin and pembrolizumab helps patients with muscle-invasive bladder cancer live longer while reducing the risk that their cancer will return after surgery, according to an international phase 3 clinical trial published in The New England Journal of Medicine.

mix_items.title: Why Europa's hidden ocean may be more difficult to reach than scientists thought
mix_items.summary: Europa, one of Jupiter's icy moons, has long fascinated scientists because of what may lie beneath its frozen shell: a global ocean of liquid water. That hidden ocean has made Europa one of the most compelling places in the solar system to study the conditions that might support life. But new research led by Rutgers scientist Lujendra Ojha suggests one of the most promising shortcuts to Europa's ocean may be far more complicated than previously thought.

mix_items.title: Red meat-related gut byproduct tied to higher atrial fibrillation risk
mix_items.summary: A Cleveland Clinic-led study shows a connection between elevated blood levels of TMAO (trimethylamine N-oxide)—a byproduct of gut bacteria digestion of nutrients found in red meat and other animal products—and a higher risk of atrial fibrillation.

mix_items.title: Io's volcanic output offers a 40‑day forecast of plasma density in Jupiter's magnetosphere
mix_items.summary: A careful statistical comparison of observations recorded by NASA's Juno mission and the Planetary Science Institute's Io Input Output observatory (IoIO) shows that IoIO observations reliably predict the density of Jupiter's plasma disk, an important part of Jupiter's magnetosphere, according to a new Geophysical Research Letters paper co-authored by Planetary Science Institute Senior Scientist Jeff Morgenthaler. Jian-Zhao Wang of the University of Colorado Boulder is the paper's lead author.

mix_items.title: Researchers develop AI-driven method to improve forecasts weeks in advance
mix_items.summary: Haonan Chen, a CSU associate professor of electrical and computer engineering, and his team have published research that could improve the accuracy of precipitation forecasts up to two weeks in advance.

mix_items.title: NASA to Support Blue Origin New Glenn Rocket Testing, Advance Artemis
mix_items.summary: NASA and Blue Origin announced Friday they recently agreed to conduct second stage hot fire testing for the company’s New Glenn rocket on the B-2 test stand at the agency’s Stennis Space Center near Bay St. Louis, Mississippi. The annex to a reimbursable Space Act Agreement signed earlier this month highlights NASA’s commitment to working […]

mix_items.title: Biomarker discovery could advance treatments for obesity-related health risks
mix_items.summary: Researchers have identified a biological marker that rises and falls as body fat changes, offering new insight into how weight gain and weight loss may influence metabolic health.

mix_items.title: Why evolution never produced mammal teeth that slice and crush equally well
mix_items.summary: Evolution is all about trade-offs. Traits that may be beneficial in one situation may backfire in another. Over millions of years, species can become specialized for one way of living rather than being equally good at everything. This explains why mammal teeth look the way they do today.

mix_items.title: UK in pole position to lead key instrument in NASA's hunt for Earth-like worlds
mix_items.summary: UK astronomers are a step closer to playing a leading role in NASA's search for alien life in the coming decades as they continue developing one of the core instruments of the Habitable Worlds Observatory (HWO). The exciting mission—the U.S. space agency's next flagship space telescope after the Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope—is expected to launch in the 2040s.

mix_items.title: Metal-ion coating improves extracellular vesicles for cancer detection and targeted delivery
mix_items.summary: Researchers, including those from the University of Tokyo, found a way to optimize how cells bind to small packages they release called extracellular vesicles. By coating the vesicles with metal ions, they made cells and their corresponding vesicles stick together more strongly than they would naturally. This reduced the time needed for cells to capture their own vesicles, even in mixtures containing billions of other vesicles.

mix_items.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
mix_items.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

mix_items.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
mix_items.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

mix_items.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
mix_items.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

mix_items.title: A tiny universe in a bottle reveals clues to the origins of life
mix_items.summary: Researchers have created cosmic dust from scratch by recreating space-like conditions inside glass tubes. The dust contains complex carbon-rich molecules built from elements essential to life and produces infrared signals similar to real material found in space. By studying these laboratory samples, scientists can explore how organic chemistry unfolds around stars and how comets, asteroids and meteorites may have carried those ingredients to Earth.

mix_items.title: The mysterious hum heard around the world may finally have an explanation
mix_items.summary: The mysterious sound known as The Hum has been reported worldwide for decades, yet its source often remains impossible to find. Researchers tested whether people who hear it have exceptional low-frequency hearing or measurable sounds produced inside their ears, but neither explanation fit most participants. The strongest possibility is that many cases involve low-frequency tinnitus, even though the sound seems completely external.

mix_items.title: Popular weight-loss drugs Ozempic and Wegovy may slow biological aging
mix_items.summary: Researchers found that semaglutide, the active ingredient in Ozempic and Wegovy, slowed biological aging markers in adults with HIV, marking the first clinical evidence that the drug may influence human aging. Although the findings are encouraging, scientists say larger studies are needed before concluding that the medication can help people age more slowly.

mix_items.title: What positive change have you seen unfold over many years?
mix_items.summary: Share the stories of change you’ve witnessed that took patience, persistence and time The post What positive change have you seen unfold over many years? appeared first on Positive News .

mix_items.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
mix_items.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

mix_items.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
mix_items.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

mix_items.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
mix_items.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

mix_items.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
mix_items.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

mix_items.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
mix_items.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

joke_ro.text: Am deschis frigiderul doar de trei ori. Progres emotional.

satire_ro.title: România a cerut extrădarea fraților Tate, ca să le dea drumul din nou pe străzi
